data.title: Wingstop's explosive growth continues -- just not its share price
data.description:
In a step closer to its ultimate goal of over 10,000 restaurants worldwide, Wingstop (WING) announced the opening of its 3,000th restaurant, expanding its global footprint with 1,000 more restaurants in just two years.

The company now operates in 47 U.S. states and 15 countries, entering six new markets since 2023 including Australia, Bahrain, Kuwait, Puerto Rico, Saudi Arabia, and The Netherlands.

“With a record pipeline of sold restaurant commitments, Wingstop shows no sign of slowing down,” CEO Michael Skipworth said.

During the company’s most recent earnings call, Skipworth reiterated Wingstop’s (WING) goal of accelerating its global footprint. During the first three quarters of 2025, Wingstop (WING) opened 369 net new restaurants, representing a 19%-unit growth rate.

“We are now opening more than 1 Wingstop per day,” Skipworth said on the call. “The demand from our brand partners is as strong as it’s ever been. In our most recent quarter, over 70 unique brand partners opened a Wingstop in over 100 different markets across the U.S.”

The explosive growth of the brand is a testament to the success of its AI-powered Smart Kitchen technology, effective marketing campaign to create viral TikTok content, and unique flavors that continue to draw in loyal customers over competitors like Buffalo Wild Wings. With a refined recipe and a singular focus on chicken wings, Wingstop (WING) regularly earns a top place in consumer rankings for best wings.

This was reflected in the company’s most recent quarterly results in which a 72.8% surge in digital sales powered a 10% increase in revenue.

Shares, however, have been beaten down over the past four months, losing as much as 43% in value since August, a result many analysts blame on the soft macro-economic environment that disproportionally weighs on restaurants.  

“Being a part of the consumer discretionary sector, WING is not immune to the macro environment,” writes Seeking Alpha analyst Pure Analytics.

Adds Seeking Alpha analyst Gary Alexander, “wings are fairly easy to find in grocery stores and make at home, so it’s not a major surprise that macro trends are pushing Wingstop’s [comparable store] sales down.”

While Wall Street analysts maintain a Buy rating on Wingstop (WING), Seeking Alpha authors are more cautious—rating it a Hold, with SA’s Quant rating even listing it as a Sell due to a weak valuation score. Pure Analytics underscores this concern, noting that the company trades at 36.3x EV/EBITDA, well above the peer median of 28.74x and its own FY22 multiple of 32.54x, and suggests a fair price range of ~$216-$235 given expected margin pressure.

Reflecting this caution, Gary Alexander says he'd like to see valuation multiples drop further into the mid-to-high teens—more in line with McDonald’s (MCD) and Domino’s (DPZ)—or a clear path to accelerating same-store sales before considering an investment.

data.title: Yatra appoints Siddhartha Gupta CEO
data.description:
Yatra Online (YTRA) announced on Wednesday the appointment of Siddhartha Gupta as CEO, effective November 25.

The India-based online travel company has elevated CEO and Co-Founder Dhruv Shringi to the post of executive board chairman.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Wednesday: URBN, DELL, AMBA, and more
data.description:
Stock futures inched up Wednesday morning as softer economic readings strengthened expectations for an imminent interest rate cut, encouraging investors to cautiously add exposure after the recent data-driven pullback.

Here are some of Wednesday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Urban Outfitters (URBN): 12.4% - Led by strength in its namesake banner and its digital channel, the company reached record sales and record net income for the third quarter, beating Wall Street’s expectations. At +12.5%, comparable sales for the Urban Outfitters banner were 3 times better than anticipated, while Anthropologie comparable sales of 7.6% also beat 4.96% expectations.

  • Autodesk (ADSK) +7.3% - Shares soared following strong Q3 results and a positive outlook, with revenue increasing 18%, driven by a 19% rise in the design segment and broad double-digit growth across all regions. The company expects Q4 revenue between $1.907B and $1.917B, surpassing the consensus of $1.87B, and adjusted EPS guidance of $2.59 to $2.67 against a consensus of $2.59. For FY2025, Autodesk projects revenue of $7.15B to $7.165B, vs. consensus of $7.1B. Adjusted EPS was forecasted between $10.18 and $10.25 for the year, above the consensus of $10.22.

  • Dell Technologies (DELL) +2.1% - Shares rose after the PC and IT giant raised its full-year sales outlook on the back of strong Q3 results and growing demand for AI servers. For FY2026, Dell now expects revenue of $111.2B–$112.2B, up 17% Y/Y at the midpoint and above both its prior $105B–$109B range and the $107.94B consensus, including $25B from AI servers (up from $20B). For Q4, the company projects revenue of $31B–$32B, topping the $27.64B consensus, and sees adjusted EPS of roughly $3.50 per share vs. analysts’ $3.31 forecast.

Biggest stock losers

  • Nutanix (NTNX) -18% - Shares plunged after the company delivered lower-than-expected guidance, forecasting FQ2 revenue of $705M to $715M (below the $749M estimate) and lowering its full-year revenue projection to $2.82B to $2.86B. While the company's CFO noted that underlying demand remains strong, the decline was attributed to a revenue shift from FQ1 into future periods, a dynamic factored into the lowered outlook, despite the firm raising its full-year free cash flow guidance.
  • Zscaler (ZS) -11% - Shares tumbled despite beating FQ1 results as the company reported an operating loss of $36.4M, which was 5% of revenue. Zscaler expects FQ2 revenue to range from $797M to $799M, above the $796.1M consensus, and adjusted EPS to range from $0.89 to $0.90, which is in line with the $0.89 estimate. The company expects full-year revenue to range from $3.28B to $3.3B, which clears the $3.27B consensus. It projects full-year adjusted EPS ranging from $3.78 to $3.82, which also hurdles the $3.65 estimate.
  • Workday (WDAY) -8.2% - Shares dipped after the back-office software provider reported Q3 results and guidance that were above Wall Street's forecast and upped its full-year forecast. For the current quarter, the back-office software provider projected subscription revenue of $2.355B (+16% Y/Y), and an adjusted operating margin of at least 28.5%. For FY2025, Workday now expects subscription revenues of $8.828B and an operating margin of approximately 29%.
  • Ambarella (AMBA) -14% - Stock fell despite third-quarter results beating estimates, which largely drew positive reactions from analysts. Morgan Stanley maintained its overweight rating on the shares with a $96 price target, noting that Internet of Things, or IoT, execution delivered another solid quarter.

More on related stocks:

  • Dell Technologies: Only One Issue Keeps The Stock From Taking Off (Earnings Review)
  • Zscaler, Inc. (ZS) Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript
  • Workday, Inc. 2026 Q3 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
  • Autodesk shares rise after quarterly beat, strong FY26 guidance
  • Zscaler targets $0.5B AI security ARR by year-end as Zero Trust initiatives accelerate

data.title: Rachel Reeves' fiscal buffer doubles in leaked budget report
data.description:
A premature leak Wednesday from the Office for Budget Responsibility has revealed that Chancellor of the Exchequer Rachel Reeves boosted the U.K.'s projected fiscal buffer to £22B ($29B) from £9.9B in March. That marks the most fiscal headroom since March 2022 under former chancellor Rishi Sunak.

The leak was described as a technical error by the OBR and is being investigated, it said, as the budget analysis was unexpectedly published minutes before Reeves delivered her formal budget speech. With the larger buffer against the U.K.'s debt limits, the official forecasts also showed a £29.8B climb in taxes.

U.K. financial stocks drifted higher in the U.S. morning trading session, with Barclays (BCS) advancing 3.5%, HSBC Holdings (HSBC) +1.3%, Lloyds Banking Group (LYG) +3.6% and NatWest Group (NWG) +3.1%. Overall, the large-cap FTSE 100 Index (UKX) gained 0.8%.

Among some of the measures in the leaked OBR document include an extra tax on homes valued at least £2M, as well as a two-percentage-point increase in property and savings income taxes. 

In addition, from 2028, only the first £2K of pension contribution will avoid National Insurance Contributions, as higher employer NIC thresholds are set to remain unchanged for three years. The government will also scale back allowances for businesses, a move expected to generate £1.5B.

data.title: Kimberly-Clark looks to Vietnam amid the global baby birth rate slump
data.description:
Kimberly-Clark Corporation (KMB) designated Vietnam as a key manufacturing hub for its Huggies diapers and other personal care products in Southeast Asia. The decision reflects both the nation's growing consumer market and its importance in the household product company’s global supply chain realignment. 

The company is concentrating production in Vietnam to serve not only the local market but also neighboring countries, leveraging lower costs, improving infrastructure, and favorable trade access in ASEAN. Notably, Vietnam's baby diaper market is expanding steadily, supported by rising incomes, urbanization, and increased hygiene awareness, with recent research projecting mid- to high-single-digit annual volume growth over the coming years.

An important factor is that Kimberly-Clark (KMB) has acknowledged that the global baby boom is over and seeks growth in emerging Asian markets, where it is trying to close the gap with market leader Unicharm (OTCPK:UNICY) and Procter & Gamble (PG). Using Vietnam as a factory hub signals a long-term bet on Southeast Asia’s consumer growth even as birth rates slow, with the strategy hinging on scale, cost efficiency, and the ability to out-innovate rivals in a crowded diaper landscape.

data.title: Wall Street rises on fresh economic signals ahead of Thanksgiving
data.description:
Wall Street watched stocks climb higher on Wednesday, with traders and investors digesting new economic data amid ongoing rate-cut speculation.

The blue-chip Dow (DJI) which rallied north of 650 points on Tuesday was last higher by 0.3%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) pushed up by 0.3% and the tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) advanced by 0.3%. 

Looking at the S&P 500 by sector, nine of the 11 segments are trending higher, with Utilities leading the gains. On the downside, Communication Services has been the weakest performing area so far. 

Treasury yields advanced as the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) moved higher by 3 basis points to 4.03% and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 4 basis points to 3.49%. 

On the economic front, initial jobless claims surprised lower in the week ended November 22. Durable goods orders slowed in September, as expected. 

“Stocks are celebrating the likelihood of a December rate cut, as it's becoming clear that the labor market is the priority for the Federal Reserve, and another rate cut would help to protect against any further cooling of the job market,” Paul Stanley, chief investment officer, Granite Bay Wealth Management stated. 

“It's becoming more clear that the stock market declines seen over most of November were simply a pullback after an extremely strong run in October and September,” Stanley added. 

As for stocks that are on the move, shares of Robinhood Markets (HOOD) tracked higher by 6.2% on the day while shares of Zscaler (ZS) dropped 11.5%.

For reference, markets will be closed on November 27, Thursday, for the Thanksgiving holiday.

  • Thanksgiving leftovers no one wants: 10 worst S&P 500 stocks of 2025

data.title: Plymouth Industrial REIT falls after Sixth Street abandons earlier proposal
data.description:
Plymouth Industrial REIT (PLYM) dropped 2.4% after Sixth Street Partners said it's abandoning its previously announced non-binding proposal to acquire the company.

Sixth Street said it no longer intends to engage in discussions or negotiations regarding its potential acquisition of Plymouth Industrial REIT (PLYM), the investor said in a  13D/A filing on Wednesday. 

The disclosure comes after Plymouth Industrial (PLYM) last month agreed to be sold to Makarora Management, along with Ares Alternative Credit funds, in an all-cash deal valued at about $2.1 billion, or $22 a share. 

Sixth Street Partners originally made a non-binding proposal to buy Plymouth Industrial (PLYM) for $24.10 a share in August.

data.title: Chicago PMI unexpectedly falls deeper into contraction in November
data.description: The Chicago PMI dropped to 36.3 in November from 43.8 in the prior month, compared with the 44.3 consensus, according to data released by the Institute for Supply Management on Wednesday.

data.title: Patria Investments to buy 51% stake in Brazilian investment manager Solis
data.description:
Patria Investments (PAX) on Wednesday announced an agreement to acquire 51% of the Brazilian investment manager Solis Investimentos.

The Cayman Islands-based alternative asset manager did not disclose financial terms of the transaction.

The addition is expected to increase Patria's total credit fee-earning AUM by over 40% to more than $11.7 pro forma as of Q3 2025.

Solis's team is set to remain in their current roles, with offices in Fortaleza and São Paulo, Brazil.

data.title: Venture Global says Shell waging campaign to damage LNG business
data.description:
Venture Global (VG) has accused Shell (SHEL) of waging a "three-year campaign" to damage its liquefied natural gas business after Shell appealed an arbitration loss, the Financial Times reported Wednesday.

In an email to employees obtained by FT, Venture Global (VG) co-founders Michael Sabel and Robert Pender blasted Shell's (SHEL) "misguided decision" to appeal the arbitration loss, saying the action "relies on completely baseless claims and is an unfortunate continuation of their three-year campaign to damage Venture Global. This behavior should be very concerning to Shell's employees, board of directors and shareholders."

Venture Global (VG) defeated Shell's (SHEL) arbitration claim at the International Chamber of Commerce, but in October it lost a separate case against BP (BP), which is seeking more than $1 billion in damages; earlier this month, Shell challenged its arbitration defeat in a New York state court in an attempt to overturn the panel’s finding.

Shell (SHEL), BP (BP) and other European companies alleged Venture Global (VG) breached supply contracts by failing to deliver shipments under long-term supply contracts, instead selling them on the spot market to profit from higher prices triggered by Russia’s 2022 invasion of
Ukraine.

Venture Global's (VG) legal difficulties have not limited its ability to sign new supply deals, with the company announcing late Tuesday that Tokyo Gas agreed to buy 1 million tons/year of LNG over 20 years, starting from 2030 - the most recent of several supply deals.
